reasoning: Recent marathon talks in Pakistan on April 11-12, 2026, involving major figures like U.S. VP JD Vance, Steve Witkoff, and Iranian FM Abbas Araghchi, have completely failed. The US delegation has left Pakistan, with Vance stating that Iran refused to make an affirmative commitment regarding nuclear weapons development. The nations have been in an active conflict for 7 weeks, and the failure of this summit puts a temporary two-week ceasefire in jeopardy, increasing the likelihood of renewed military escalation. Furthermore, the market criteria explicitly rule out partial agreements or frameworks—the deal must be a formal, binding accord with verifiable commitments on enrichment and stockpiles. Negotiating such a complex technical agreement typically takes many months or years. The collapse of these high-stakes face-to-face talks leaves virtually no runway to achieve a comprehensive, finalized, and publicly confirmed formal agreement by the tight June 30, 2026, deadline. I therefore estimate the probability to be exceptionally low.
